metadata.sources: Remodeling Magazine 2026 Cost vs Value, James Hardie installer pricing, LP SmartSide installer pricing, CertainTeed/Mastic/Royal vinyl distributor pricing, RSMeans residential 2026, manufacturer warranty docs

materialTiers.label: Vinyl
materialTiers.climateFitness: Best in mild and cold climates. Warps under direct south/west sun in hot climates above ~95F sustained. Color fade typical after 12-18 years.
materialTiers.insulationOption: Insulated vinyl (foam-backed) adds R-2 to R-3.5 and reduces oil-canning. Adds $1.50-$3/sqft.

materialTiers.label: Fiber cement
materialTiers.climateFitness: Best all-around: handles heat, cold, freeze-thaw, and salt air. The 2026 default for hot-humid and coastal regions. Heavy (250 lb per square) so requires sturdy framing.
materialTiers.insulationOption: Rainscreen detail (1/4 to 3/4 inch furring) significantly extends life and improves drying. Adds $1-$2/sqft.

materialTiers.label: Engineered wood
materialTiers.climateFitness: Best in mixed and cold climates. Treated for termites and moisture. Looks closer to real wood than vinyl. Less brittle than fiber cement in cold installs.
materialTiers.insulationOption: Most installs are over rigid foam or rainscreen for moisture management.

materialTiers.label: Real wood (cedar, redwood, pine)
materialTiers.climateFitness: Requires re-staining or sealing every 3-5 years. Not recommended in coastal high-humidity unless you accept aggressive maintenance.
materialTiers.insulationOption: Often paired with rainscreen for the look while extending lifespan.

materialTiers.label: Stucco (3-coat traditional)
materialTiers.climateFitness: Best in hot-dry and Mediterranean climates. Cracks track foundation movement so expansive-soil regions require control joints every 144 sqft.

materialTiers.label: Synthetic stucco / EIFS
materialTiers.climateFitness: Drainage-grade EIFS only — old face-sealed EIFS is the source of widespread moisture damage and many class actions. Verify drainage assembly.

materialTiers.label: Metal (steel, aluminum)
materialTiers.climateFitness: Excellent for hail-prone and wildfire areas. Heat-soaked in deep south. Quiet with proper underlayment.

materialTiers.label: Stone or brick veneer (manufactured)
materialTiers.climateFitness: Best as accent (front facade, water table). Full wraps are expensive and require integral flashing.

vinyl_full_house.label: Vinyl siding (whole house, ~1,500-2,200 sqft of wall area)
vinyl_full_house.notes: Includes tear-off, house wrap, J-channel, soffit and fascia where same-material. Insulated vinyl adds 25-40%.

fiber_cement_full_house.label: Fiber cement (HardiePlank) whole house
fiber_cement_full_house.notes: Heavy material, more labor. ColorPlus pre-finished saves field-paint cost. Lap is the standard plank profile.

engineered_wood_full_house.label: Engineered wood (LP SmartSide) whole house
engineered_wood_full_house.notes: Most homes go pre-finished (Diamond Kote / ExpertFinish) to lock the warranty.

wood_full_house.label: Real cedar or redwood whole house
wood_full_house.notes: Cedar shingles and shakes carry a premium over bevel siding.

stucco_full_house.label: Traditional stucco whole house
stucco_full_house.notes: 3-coat application over wire lath. Color in finish coat is the long-life choice.

stone_veneer_accent.label: Manufactured stone veneer (accent wall, ~250 sqft)
stone_veneer_accent.notes: Front facade or water table accent. Requires drainage plane and weep screed.

partial_repair.label: Partial siding repair (storm or rot)
partial_repair.notes: Match-finding is the hard part on aged vinyl — weathered colors rarely match factory-new pieces.

remove_and_install_premium.label: Remove asbestos siding and replace with new
remove_and_install_premium.notes: Asbestos abatement adds $3-$6/sqft on top of new siding cost. Must be done by licensed abatement contractor with disposal manifest.

supportingComponents.item: House wrap (Tyvek HomeWrap, ZIP System tape, or equivalent)
supportingComponents.notes: Code-required when siding comes off. Skipping is grounds to walk away.
Adds R-3 to R-6 plus thermal break. Required by IECC in some climate zones.
Doubles drying capacity behind cladding. Required by some manufacturer warranties (James Hardie HZ5 in some climates).
Aluminum or vented vinyl. Almost always needed when redoing siding.
Aluminum capping or PVC trim around openings.
Skipping kick-out flashing where roof meets wall is the #1 cause of post-install rot.

supportingComponents.item: Lead paint test and abatement (pre-1978 homes)
supportingComponents.notes: EPA RRP rule. Verify the contractor is RRP-certified before starting work on a pre-1978 home.

permitCosts.notes: Many jurisdictions require a building permit for re-siding. Almost all require one if any structural work is involved.

stateLicensing.notes: Siding installers are typically state-licensed under the general contractor license. A few states have a specialty home improvement license.

codeReferences.iccCode: 2024 IRC where adopted; 2021 IRC in lagging states
codeReferences.weatherResistiveBarrier: IRC R703.1 — water-resistive barrier (house wrap or equivalent) required behind siding except for vinyl in some jurisdictions
codeReferences.flashing: IRC R703.4 — kick-out, head, and pan flashing at all penetrations
codeReferences.fireRatedSiding: IBC Chapter 7 — non-combustible cladding required in WUI zones (CA Title 24 Chapter 7A is the strictest)
codeReferences.energyCode: 2021 IECC requires continuous insulation in many climate zones; rigid foam under siding is the common path
codeReferences.leadPaint: EPA RRP rule — pre-1978 homes require RRP-certified contractor for any siding work that disturbs paint
codeReferences.windRating: ASCE 7 wind zones — coastal counties often require Miami-Dade or TDI windborne-debris-rated installations

redFlagPatterns.explanation: Siding tear-off without explicit mention of new house wrap (WRB). Code requires it (IRC R703.1). Skipping it leads to wall rot within 5-10 years. Walk away or demand it in writing.

No mention of flashing at windows, doors, and roof-wall intersections. Missing kick-out flashing is the #1 cause of post-install moisture damage. Reputable installers always document the flashing detail.

Pre-1978 home or asbestos mentioned with no EPA RRP certification or lead-safe protocol. Federal law requires RRP-certified contractors for paint-disturbing work on pre-1978 homes. Fines run $37,000+ per violation per day. Verify certification at epa.gov.

Asbestos siding mentioned but no licensed abatement contractor or disposal protocol. Asbestos siding removal requires a licensed abatement contractor in nearly every state. DIY removal violates EPA rules.

James Hardie fiber cement quote with no mention of rainscreen or furring. The current James Hardie HZ5 installation requirements (in many climates) require a drainage gap behind the siding to maintain warranty. Without it the warranty may be voided.
Siding line items with no brand or material grade named. Vinyl ranges $4-$10/sqft; fiber cement $9-$18; cedar $9-$22 — without a brand you cannot benchmark fairness. Reputable quotes always specify.
Quote with no mention of permits. Many jurisdictions require a building permit for full re-siding. Unpermitted work voids homeowner insurance and creates liability at sale.
Quote installs new siding over existing siding without tear-off. Overlay traps moisture, hides rot, and voids most manufacturer warranties. Always specify full tear-off.
No mention of contractor license number. Most states require a state license for siding work above a dollar threshold. Verify at your state's contractor board before signing.
Re-siding in a cold climate with no continuous insulation. The IECC requires continuous insulation in zones 4-8. This is the moment to add R-5+ rigid foam — it will not be cheaper at any other time.
No workmanship warranty stated. Reputable siding installers offer 5-25 year workmanship warranties separate from the manufacturer material warranty.

Partial siding repair with no color-match disclosure. Aged vinyl and painted wood do not match factory-fresh replacement pieces. Reputable installers disclose this up front and offer alternatives like blending.
Deposits above 25% on siding work are high risk. Many states cap deposits by law. Reputable installers stage payments with material delivery and substantial completion milestones.
Tear-off disposal not mentioned. A whole-house siding tear-off generates 4-8 cubic yards of debris. Dumpster rental adds $400-$900.
Coastal location implied but no Miami-Dade / TDI / WPI-8 windborne-debris assembly mentioned. Coastal counties require windborne-rated installations or insurance won't cover wind damage.

scopeCheckItems.label: Siding brand and product line specified
Permits identified and pulled by contractor

iraCredit.notes: Siding is not a federal-credit-eligible category. Continuous insulation added during a re-side could have qualified under 25C in 2025; that credit expired Dec 31 2025. Some state utility rebates still apply to insulation upgrades — check dsireusa.org.

utilityRebates.notes: Stacks during re-side projects with R-5+ continuous insulation.

questionsToAskContractor: What brand and product line of siding are you quoting? Show me the spec sheet.
Is this a tear-off or overlay? (Walk away if overlay.)
What house wrap / WRB are you installing and how is it lapped at openings?
Where will you install kick-out flashing where the roof meets the wall?
Is the home pre-1978? If so, are you EPA RRP certified?
Does the quote include rainscreen or furring strips for fiber cement?
What is the workmanship warranty separate from the material warranty?
Are you adding continuous rigid foam insulation? Why or why not for my climate zone?
What state contractor license number do you carry, and when does it expire?
What permits are required and who pulls them?
How is soffit and fascia replacement handled and at what cost?
What is the disposal plan for tear-off debris?
